title: Troubling times: a comment on Judith Butler's ‘Sexual politics, torture and secular time’
abstract: These are ‘troubled times’. For some, such troubles are felt immediately in and on the body, for others they are filtered through media and representations. But it is not only the ‘trouble’ itself that forms the object of analysis for Judith Butler's paper, it is also the ‘times’; and the plurality of the temporal is key. For Butler, it might be said that time itself, specifically secular time, is the trouble; but a different conception of time might be part of thinking towards resolutions. In this paper, Butler continues her project of a politically significant, socially informed, philosophical intervention into the issue of sexual politics. Her earlier, groundbreaking work on gender and the performative has literally transformed an existing field of sociological enquiry and inaugurated another, that of queer theory. Her insistence on the embodied materiality of discourse has provided a rich source for scholars interested in identification and subjectivation. Here, the tortured body comes into focus through Butler's exploration of the ways in which a neo-liberal discourse of freedom becomes the method by which unfreedoms are secured. Throughout this paper Butler undermines the tendencies of certain Northern states to construct a culturally unified ‘West’ against a monolithic, terrible Islam with a typically sophisticated comparison of the similarities between the two. The paper illustrates some of the ways in which the secular has become linked to the ‘non-religious’, and the ‘anti-religious’ often functions as code for ‘anti-Islam’. The ‘freedoms of modern, secular culture’ then can form the basis for xenophobic policies and practices, such as those governing immigration to certain European countries. Butler argues that the problem of time is one that is familiar in current debates about US imperialism. In this account, modernity is only knowable through its own conceptions of time as linear and progressive. Butler is not so much concerned with the debates about what modernity might actually be, but is more interested in the way in which a discourse of ‘modernity’ functions. The ‘conceit of progress’ that informs discussions of the emergence of modernity and founds the concept of civilization also authorizes the recurrence of the need for (military) interventions around the globe. To be modern is to be beyond the pre-modern; to be modern is to be after the traditional, to be forward looking and thinking. Such futurity is, needless to say, a mark of civilization that exceeds the primitive and the backward. In such conceptions, temporality is also spatialized, and territories of past time need to be liberated. Most importantly she suggests the mark of modernity is freedom, which most often appears to take the form of democracy. Democratic liberation must be brought to those who are enslaved by systems of belief and social and political organization which found the oppression of certain members of society. The relationship between time and space organizes bodies and cultures into sites requiring intervention. In this analysis, Butler's work echoes those anti- and postcolonial scholars who interrogate/d the ways in which European imperialism and colonialism operate/d; and in its insistence upon the centrality of sexual politics resonates with feminist and queer postcolonialism. Huntingdon and Friedman, for example, evoke a call and response from Senghor, Manoni and Fanon to Said, Bhabha and Spivak. The explicit reference to a ‘civilising mission’ stands in stark contrast to the denial that such things could have ever happened before. If the ‘character’ of ‘the primitive’ is marred by the lack of certain attributes, it is of course off-set by an excess of others. The paper considers the role of patriarchy in these terms. Butler suggests that in France the ‘problem of the immigrant’ (here taken to mean North African Muslims) is an excess of both religious fervour and ‘culture’ but is also impeded by the lack of proper patriarchal control. In her lecture, however, Butler also noted that when referring to women who veil, it was the excess of patriarchy that was the problem. Such an analysis points to the impossibilities for ‘the migrant’ to become a subject of this modern state. Yet Butler rightly shows up the ways in which this process of making foreign comes from a nation-state in which heterosexual kinship is the ordering principle for citizenship and in which fathers provide both literal and symbolic power. In this case it is not simply the quantity of patriarchy that is wrong – it is the quality of patriarchy that is troubling. This wrongness is ‘traditional’ and not modern, and therefore a non-benevolent form of authority. Here again we see a temporal as well as territorial wrongness. However, ‘traditional values’ are being resurrected in the (sexual) politics of, for example, the UK. One part of this debate suggests that gay men and lesbians (other ‘sexual minorities’ do not often figure here) are only acceptable as long as they emulate ‘traditional’, for that read heterosexual, nuclear family forms. This can be achieved through state sanctioned partnership, if not fully recognized through the intertwining of religion and state which is recognizable as marriage. We cannot simply call this is a reflexive modernity; it appears as something more like a Janus-faced form of time rather than nation. Janus was, of course, the mythical ‘keeper of the gate’ and this gate is the entry to modern subjecthood, something of an ambivalent prize at best. The troubling aspects of modern sexual politics are particularly evident in the practice of and spectacularization of torture. In her meticulous analysis of the bodies of torture, Butler shows the relationship between the hyper-masculinism of the US army and those of the tortured populations of Iraqi prisoners – the men in particular. Here again Butler carefully shows the way in which sexual politics underpin similarities in the ways in which ‘the Arab’ is characterized, and the US soldiers are behaving. The signs of the barbarian – misogyny and homophobia, violence and torture – become the tools of the civilized. The masculinities and femininities that are imagined are formed through conventional understandings of the ‘Orientalized Other’, a simplistic and problematic terminology, but one that holds some relevance here. Butler's analysis points to the further complexity in insisting on a feminist and queer response to violence that has through the misdirection of some been seen as, in part, the outcome of modern sexual political progression. In many responses to these acts, it is hard to escape a logic of hegemonic, racialized heterogender. The men are sexual aggressors (the prisoners tamed, the soldiers active). The women prisoners are objects to be unveiled, yet paradoxically not revealed to the US public as they threaten the logic of the war. The (white) female soldier portrayed in images of torture and degradation becomes masculine (but feminized by a male higher authority); the male ‘Arab’ prisoners, feminized. These common readings are effective, but draw upon stereotypical views of racialized sexual schemas. The binaries are all there and intact, male/female; white/brown. As such they are framed in a structurally tautological raced, sexual and gender politics which occludes the more complicated ways in which power, sex and violence operate in concert with racialization, ‘Orientalism’ and ‘Islamophobia’, seemingly of the past but profoundly of the now. In addition, the tortured body is not no-body. It is in fact some-body. Here Guantanamo and Abu Ghraib show different facets of the fixing of ‘the Muslim’. In Guantanamo, the prisoners are outside of time. A key feature of the torture is de-humanization through complete sensory deprivation. Prisoners know no time but an endless now of subjugation. In Abu Ghraib the somebodies are anonymized and distilled into a mere handful of ‘iconic images’ that come to represent the totality of the torture, and through the release of the images, the control of an entire group labelled ‘Arab Muslims’. These anonymous men are denied an agency in the telling of the story of their own very real torture; but they are, to mis-quote Fanon, somehow responsible at the same time for their body, ‘race’ and ancestors; ‘battered down’ by the stereotypes and caricatures (Fanon 1986 [1952]: 112). These men in particular bear the weight of history as well as the pain of imperialism upon their bodies. The spectacular as a key mechanism of control adds to this ‘burden of representation’; but as many authors have noted these are methods with a history. For example there are continuities between the pictures from Abu Ghraib and the systematic torture and killings of African American men and the subsequent reproduction of images of lynchings (Carby 2004). Towards the end of her paper Butler calls for a political alliance that neither homogenizes modern ‘Western’ (European?) culture such as French or Dutch, nor that of (supra-national?) Islam. She rightly argues that this process of simplification denies the extended debates amongst for example, Muslim women who might identify as feminist, or those who might call themselves Islamic feminists. In the UK, the rising tide of ‘Islamophobia’, the fear of the ‘home grown terrorist’ and the encroachment on human rights in the name of ‘security’ have led to discussions about the failure of the ‘secular left’ to provide responses and the failure of coalitional politics in general. The naming of such a project is in itself problematic. For those who align themselves with terms such as ‘feminist’, ‘queer’ and ‘postcolonial’ these issues still require a shared political project and willingness to collectivize under these signs (Ali 2007). In claiming alliance to postcolonial feminist and queer politics one claims allegiance to issues that foreground the gendered nature of a geopolitics of capitalist exploitation and domination by what Mohanty (2003) has called the one third that control the two thirds. Butler's paper functions as an exemplar of what it seeks to explain. Throughout, the spectres of the past, of the previous acts of torture and military expansionism by US and European forces, press themselves into mind. This is not to argue that things are just as they were, that the technologies of governmentality may not have been enhanced by, for example, the introduction of digital media and the internet. However the feeling of ‘déjà vu’ is overwhelming. This is as it should be. It is the histories of the present that found feminist and queer postcolonial work, and here the convergence with Benjamin's time is most evident. The now becomes a form of temporality that is inescapably the not past or future. It echoes the form of psychoanalytic time, that is time out of place, out of order, erupting into the present from a past forgotten or repressed. The past appears in the present in ways that we could not have envisioned nor imagined. Butler's own work on melancholia has shown this, and has been fruitfully extended to include an analysis of empire by Paul Gilroy (2004). For those whose thinking emerged from recent European lineage it is not perhaps possible, after all, to think otherwise about this time and engage with the horrors of militarism and neo-imperialism. We might do well to consider clipping the wings of the angel of progress (McClintock 1992). One strategy could be to follow Sara Ahmed's (2006) move to think of an orientation to a shared object; to face the same direction, in our desire to form collectives. The outcomes might be uncertain, but the intentionality is shared. As ever, Judith Butler helps us with that task.
